title: Statistical Mechanics of Random Matrices
abstract: These lecture notes are based on the lectures on \emph{Statistical Mechanics of Random Matrices} delivered at the Spring College on the Physics of Complex Systems, held at the Abdus Salam International Centre for Theoretical Physics, Trieste, Italy, from 19 February to 15 March 2024. Their aim is to present a statistical-mechanics route to the spectral theory of sparse and diluted random matrices, with emphasis on cavity and replica methods, resolvent techniques, population dynamics, typical spectral densities, spectral-count fluctuations and large deviations, conditioned spectra, and non-Hermitian extensions. The written form of the notes has been deliberately expanded beyond the material actually covered during the lectures. This is partly because a set of lecture notes can afford a more systematic development than a sequence of blackboard lectures, and partly because several natural continuations of the material become clearer once the central methods have been introduced. Consequently, not every topic discussed here was presented during the College. The additional material is included to give a more coherent account of the subject and to indicate directions that, hopefully, can be covered in greater detail in future lectures or schools. Since these are lecture notes rather than a state-of-the-art review, the choice of topics is necessarily selective and is naturally tilted towards the author's own work and collaborations on this subject. I have nevertheless tried, within the limits of this format, to place the material in contact with the broader literature and to represent the surrounding state of the art as fairly as possible. Inevitably, some relevant contributions may be missing or treated too briefly; such omissions are unintentional and reflect the pedagogical scope of the notes rather than a judgement on their importance.
summary: This work presents a rigorous theoretical framework using advanced statistical mechanics to analyze the spectra of sparse and diluted random matrices. It systematically develops methods like the cavity and replica techniques to calculate spectral density and fluctuations. This is crucial for understanding complex systems where local graph structure dictates global spectral behavior.
main_problem: The primary goal is to develop a statistical-mechanics route to compute spectral properties for sparse and diluted random matrices, moving beyond the standard dense matrix theory.
main_result: The paper establishes the mathematical equivalence between the cavity method and the replica method when calculating spectral observables like the density or large deviation functions for these sparse ensembles.
method: It employs advanced techniques from statistical mechanics, including the replica trick, the cavity method (and its associated population dynamics), and saddle-point analysis on functional integrals.
model_or_system: The focus is on random matrices derived from graphs: sparse symmetric matrices, diluted Wishart ensembles, and non-Hermitian directed/asymmetric matrices. The underlying structure is governed by graph connectivity (degrees, motifs).
key_observables: Empirical spectral density ($ho_N(\lambda)$), resolvent functions, eigenvalue counting statistics (large deviations), and conditioned spectra.
important_parameters: Sparsity parameter ($c=O(1)$ in the thermodynamic limit), graph connectivity structure (degrees $k_i$), and the replica index $n 	o 0$.
main_assumptions: The analysis often relies on the replica-symmetric ansatz, and the notes are deliberately expanded lecture material rather than a comprehensive review.
figures_summary: Figure 1 schematically illustrates support conventions for different types of diluted matrix ensembles (undirected, directed, bipartite).
paper_structure: The structure moves systematically from defining the problem in sparse graph theory to introducing advanced tools like the cavity and replica methods, culminating in demonstrating their equivalence across various spectral observables.
why_interesting: The techniques developed—especially the use of local recursion (cavity) combined with powerful statistical physics tools (replica)—are highly transferable to analyzing complex quantum systems where interactions are sparse or structured, such as disordered spin glasses or lattice models on random graphs.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Schematic support conventions for diluted matrix ensembles. In the bi- partite case, the one-mode projection shows how factor nodes generate induced variable–variable couplings.

Figure 2: Belief propagation and population dynamics as two implementations of the same cavity recursion. On a fixed locally tree-like instance, directed-edge messages are updated by removing the recipient from the incoming neighbourhood. At the ensemble level, the message law is represented by an empirical population, and the same local recursion is sampled as a Monte Carlo fixed-point equation.

Figure 3: Kesten–McKay versus the semicircle law in the dense-connectivity scaling. The finite-c curves are the analytic random-regular densities after rescaling eigenval- ues by c−1/2; the dotted curve is the Wigner semicircle law obtained when the cavity message distribution concentrates.

Figure 4: Bipartite cavity structure of a diluted Wishart factor. A sample node µ con- tributes the rank-one matrix d−1x µ(x µ)T to the covariance matrix, producing a clique in the one-mode projection but a single local factor in the bipartite representation. The factor-to-variable message Uµ→i is defined without the overall d−1 prefactor; the contribution entering a variable Green-function denominator is d−1Uµ→i.

Figure 5: Hermitization as vertex doubling and block-valued cavity messaging. The non-Hermitian problem is lifted from one scalar field per vertex to two fields (ui, vi) per vertex. The block Z i is the bare on-site precision of this doubled local pair, while Ai j gives the cross-layer coupling between doubled neighboring vertices. Conse- quently the cavity Green function sent from i to j is a 2 × 2 matrix; its lower-left entry is the component averaged to form the regularized non-Hermitian resolvent field.
